text: Throughout my 3 years of audit articles, I used CaseWare Working Papers to perform audits (documentation of all audit work performed was done in Working Papers) and draft annual financial statements. In my current role, Working Papers are mostly used for just drafting of the annual financial statements for clients. I enjoy using CaseWare Working Papers to draft financial statements, because all relevant standards are available, and templates can be easily loaded into the program when needed. In addition, annual financial statements can be entirely customized according to client preference, while taking into consideration compliance with the relevant standards. There are no issues that are noteworthy. It is just a pity that CaseWare Working Papers cannot be 100% integrated into my own audit firm's in-house developed audit software. Other than that, CaseWare Working Papers is a very good audit software.

text: Caseware is an extremely powerful and comprehensive audit and accounting platform. It excels in handling large volumes of financial data, automating working papers, and maintaining strong cross-referencing and audit trail integrity. The smart templates, structured work programmes, and built-in risk assessment tools significantly improve audit quality and consistency. The ability to link trial balances dynamically to lead schedules and financial statements is a major strength, and the collaboration features support efficient multi-user audit teams. Review collected by and hosted on G2.com. Caseware has a steep learning curve, particularly for new users. Initial setup, mapping, and template configuration can be time-consuming. Performance may slow down on very large files, especially during synchronisation. The interface is functional rather than modern, and some advanced customisations require specialist technical knowledge or vendor support. Review collected by and hosted on G2.com. Validated Reviewer Source: Organic Verified User in Accounting Verified User in Accounting Small-Business (50 or fewer emp.) 12/17/2025 "Effortless Audits with Intuitive Templates"

text: I like that I am able to see the trial balance and the working papers without closing one or the other. I like that I am able to easily create automatic reports such as grouping schedules for each audit area, I like that Caseview documents are able to be edited by staff to tweak templates to the letters we need for each client. I like being able to link balances and other engagement information into excel and word documents using Caseware connector, however this feature is probably the glitchiest of all. Review collected by and hosted on G2.com. I dislike that the formulas and formatting used within the Caseview documents do not match the equivalent function in Excel. Also, when we purchased the product, a deleted workpaper went to a place within the software called the recycle bin and could be recovered. Caseware has now done away with the recycle bin and a deleted document is gone forever. Furthermore, you cannot print a departmental income statement from an automatic document. You have to make an income statement for each department using that department's context and print them all to get a departmental picture. Smartsync repairs never work. If you have a sync issue that cannot be resolved by confirming one version over the other, there is seemingly no way to resolve it. Review collected by and hosted on G2.com. Current User Validated Reviewer Incentivized Source: G2 invite Verified User in Accounting Verified User in Accounting Enterprise (> 1000 emp.) 12/8/2017 "Phenomenal Auditing Tool "

text: Specialized for audit and assurance engagements, a very powerful document manager and project tracker that allows teams to embed files in a document tree and track progress through preparer and reviewer sign-offs. All within one easy to access window listing all the files included. It allows collaboration through multiple users being able to access the same project file at once, including over networks. The program itself is very responsive with little delays if working through an intranet or local storage. The compression feature allows all the documents in the file to be packaged into a single, smaller file for easy transfer through email, removable storage, etc. The file can be locked down once it has been completed. It also allows a sign-out or check-out feature of either the entire file or individual files for working offline or away from the master file, and the files can be signed back in at a later date. Review collected by and hosted on G2.com. As multiple users enter into a single project file, they can each edit separate embedded files but usually cannot collaborate on the same file at once. Multiple users will also slow the program down significantly, to the point of being unusable if over an internet connection. The program is also more of a file manager than file storage, meaning the files listed in the main window are only links to the actual files. Files can be deleted outside of CaseWare Working Papers and thus the links will break. There is also a chance for corruption when using the compression feature as well as when transferring or working remotely through a network connection; the program does not appear to have a fail safe for network disconnects in mid-transfer. Review collected by and hosted on G2.com. Current User Validated Reviewer Incentivized Source: G2 invite Verified User in Accounting Verified User in Accounting Mid-Market (51-1000 emp.) 1/24/2017 "Easy to use for tax accounting and returns."

There are a LOT of features packed in that are not necessary for a lot of day to day users who use it mainly for file organization. These can bog it down and also get in the way. It's a trade-off to the fact that it's relatively customizable and has a lot of options/features. Review collected by and hosted on G2.com. Validated Reviewer Incentivized Source: G2 invite Verified User in Accounting Verified User in Accounting Enterprise (> 1000 emp.) 8/26/2016 "Easy-to-Use Accounting Software"

text: Throughout my 3 years of audit articles, I used CaseWare Working Papers to perform audits (documentation of all audit work performed was done in Working Papers) and draft annual financial statements. In my current role, Working Papers are mostly used for just drafting of the annual financial statements for clients. I enjoy using CaseWare Working Papers to draft financial statements, because all relevant standards are available, and templates can be easily loaded into the program when needed. In addition, annual financial statements can be entirely customized according to client preference, while taking into consideration compliance with the relevant standards. There are no issues that are noteworthy. It is just a pity that CaseWare Working Papers cannot be 100% integrated into my own audit firm's in-house developed audit software. Other than that, CaseWare Working Papers is a very good audit software. Read Less BB Baburam B. Verified reviewer Accounting 2-10 employees Used daily for less than 12 months Review source Reviewed December 2024 CaseWare Cloud: User-Friendly and Simple 5 CaseWare Cloud offers a user-friendly interface, making navigating and generating financial statements easy. It is better software for audit and accounting firms to compile the financial information and prepare the financial statement as required by CPA Canada. We don't have to worry about anything if we are able to input the correct trial balance.

text: La capacidad para procesar archivos, permite trabajar con archivos de mas de 15 millones de registros, procesamiento rápido, las carpetas creadas para almacenar la información, facilidad para crear la consultas. Herramienta para personas no expertas en sistemas de información. La generación de estadísticas de cada archivo. La forma en que crecen exponencialmente los archivos a medida que se hacen consultas y a veces el tamaño de los archivos después de múltiples consultas no permite leerlo. Los ejemplos de la ayuda no son claros para quien quiere utilizar formulas. Read Less JM Jodi M. Verified reviewer Accounting 11-50 employees Used weekly for more than 2 years Review source Reviewed August 2019 Fantastic learning platform! 5 I have never really had training in completing and performing tax computations and tax returns other than in my studies. I have always had to learn through trial and error. Before using tax module, i may have known how to do corporate income tax but I never really understood certain rules and where things go without double checking. This all changed when I started using the tax module as it provides an adaptable platform which shows exactly where and how things make their way into the tax calculation. At first, it's quitetime consuming but once you get the hang of it, you'll never go back to your old system. Instead of sitting for hours in training or worst case scenario, following previous years as guidelines to figure out where everything goes in the tax return, you are able to identify what goes where and why. It has also assisted with turning 3 processes into 1. Everything can be done through the tax module and be exported to eFiling and your operating system.

Caseware is terrific. The Caseware linkage addon for Excel is a game changer.

Same here. If I know that I’ll have the same recurring AJEs (M&E, book-tax depreciation, officers life insurance, etc), I always link things in the workpapers to try and automate the necessary calculations. Makes things so much easier.

I went private and our department got caseware subscriptions. It's useful because we have like tens of subs we do the Financials and tax returns for.

Caseware is way better than engagement in my opinion.

I went from Caseware to CCH and then SAP. It was only downhill from Caseware

Change firms. Don’t put yourself through an audit engagement platform or methodology change. Everything will take longer and your firm will not account for the extra time every single audit will take. Was at RSM for maps to orb update (all within caseware) and it was brutal.
Caseware is pretty good already. It can get much worse.
Its not perfect, but it has some of the best features and tools for trial balance software after-the-fact.

The first time I used caseware, I had no idea what was going on or the point of what I was doing. The training at my first firm sucked and it isn't the easiest software to learn. But now, I love it and I don't even use it for most of what it is probably capable of as I work at a very small firm so my engagements are very small as well. Being able to have all documents in one place and add comments to them and check them off and have the data for working papers pulled in for you is great. I think of it as a document manager that does more and is made specifically for accounting. Having the right setup/learning the setup that's given to you is a big part of it being useful.
Didn't expect to see many comments from people that love CaseWare lol

Yah we are working through the transition from Caseware desktop with cloud storage to the full Onpoint suite for all audit types. I’m remaining optimistic, but different in numerous ways. More structured and ridged in some, but in hindsight, those were corners we were able to cut in the more flexible desktop version.
Any onpoint pros out there I can pick your brain? DM me, please I beg you!!!

We are in this boat right now, switching from desktop to cloud. I LOVED desktop. But I am kinda freaked out by how difficult the cloud version is to get used to. Everything is taking me twice as long, if not more. How is it going for you guys??
Anyone know of any good CW training materials?
100%; caseware versions and multi-user access should be standard.
Audit & Assurance I’ll take my big 4 built in-house file manager over caseware for audits any day. But I do see caseware’s effectiveness for smaller firms and review files.

i was also curious what bigger firms use, but yea for smaller firms i think caseware makes more sense, since we also have tons of clients using quickbooks so its easy to format and import things from excel
Caseware is fine I guess if your trial balance is mapped and setup properly, and you’re used to preparing returns that way.

That being said, I’m a staunch advocate for using clients QBO or otherwise it’s going in excel. Only use caseware for two massive returns per year, the rest I don’t even bother. I waste more time mapping, integrating, and relearning the software than I’d ever benefit in time saved.

A properly mapped and set up CW with a good template set is great. Such a time saver. With Jazz-it, you can take a brand new file, import the TB from QBO, map it and have financials that are 95% good within 15 minutes. Then you can export the GIFI right into your tax software with a few clicks.

I totally understand how that is a huge positive when you’ve got it operating as intended. Our firms clients just aren’t that complex of corporate returns, though. If it’s already cleaned up in QBO, I can enter the trial balance straight into ultra tax in less than 15 minutes for 90% of our corporate returns. This year we actually dropped down to 2 caseware licenses for just my partner and I because we just don’t use it enough to justify the $1,000/person license annually for the rest of our tax staff.

Audit & Assurance Currently on a pilot group with my firm for Caseware on EBP audits, to hopefully transition to the rest of audit within the next couple years. It’s definitely more complex and harder to understand than our current software (Engagement 🤮), but it is miles better in almost every other way.
Hey what do you mean. In OnPoint EBP? I'm not talking about das
Caseware is like the easiest software ive used. I loved how well thought out it was.
Now i have my own practice and just use a shitload of excel working papers that Caseware did on the back end.

I'm not entirely sure why my firm decided to rollout Caseware for our attestation services other than AICPA pushing for firms to accept it. My firm has been using both DAS and PCR for the past two years in some capacity and is transitioning to use DAS full time for the upcoming tax season. While we heard all about the benefits of DAS and PCR, most of what was celebrated turned out to not be true. It's taken a while to adjust to the new systems. While both DAS and PCR do offer some benefits compared to CCH Prosystems, there are still plenty of drawbacks.

So, the onboarding process.... it takes A LOT more effort to get up to speed with using DAS and PCR than it does for CCH Prosystems. I remember during a DAS engagement last year, the partner on the job was hoping the audit team's realization wouldn't drop below 60%. He was shocked when the senior manager, who had previous DAS experience, alerted him to the fact that our realization would likely be between 20-30%. I still have no confidence that most managers and partners realize how much time it takes to setup an engagement. For simple clients with revenues below $3M, it usually takes me a week to setup the risk assessment. Granted, I've had some experience with the software and I know the clients fairly well. For clients that have revenues over $25M and some complex accounting, the setup process can take upwards of three weeks. Expect the first few engagements to take a while. The software is not the most intuitive or user friendly. Though, version R5 is much superior to R3.

Unexpected pros..... I'm still looking for some. I still haven't identified or experienced any efficiencies in using the software. However, in terms of bottlenecks, this is mostly on the human level. If your firm has inefficient processes due to poor management, Caseware is not going to fix that.

Unexpected cons..... we were sold on Caseware in its ability to use some sort of AI or machine learning to improve efficiencies. I haven't experienced this with either DAS or PCR. Each time the Caseware folks push out an update, the update appears to break everything we had previously worked on. This was entirely true when we rolled forward previous year PCR binders. Certain workpapers were completely blank, the financial groupings were reset, and the financial statements had reverted back to template form. So yeah, instead of improving efficiencies, PCR actually made us less efficient.

Wow that sounds like a horrible experience. My firm is in the process of implementing DAS in the next couple of years. But they do have partners looking at it figuring out what they want from it. Not sure why the AICPA needs to be even involved in this (aside from the tit for tat with the endorsement of it).
I would rather us keep using Caseware Working Papers instead of the Caseware Cloud. But hey what do I know, I am just a staff.

If you guys are going to stick with it, let me recommend that you make screen shots of all the workpapers you create within DAS. Today, I opened a DAS binder to check to see if the manager had signed off on the Risk Assessment. Despite saying he had, I found that he in fact hadn't. However, when I was looking at the Risk Assessment, I noticed that certain risks were telling me to complete w/p IC-1. I found it strange because I completed the entire workpaper last week.

Anyways, I opened IC-1 to find that DAS had unlinked all my subprocesses, risks to the subprocesses, deleted all of my narratives, and all my walkthroughs reverted back to the basic templates. I lost a week's worth of work just because DAS is Ass. Plus, there are no known "undo" buttons, way to lock down workpapers, or even a basic log to identify who did what to what workpaper.
So, if you find yourself using DAS, be sure to screen shot all of your completed workpapers. It's easier to re-do it when the Caseware support folks tell you that there is nothing they can do.

Thank you so much..:this is incredibly helpful. The sales guy (who is actually horribly unprofessional, so I’m forcing myself to stay on this path already) told us we wouldn’t need DAS and wasn’t pushing it at all, but based on your experience, I’m struggling to see how the functionality in OnPoint Audit and PCR is any better if you’re running into these kinds of issues. We’re currently exploring Caseware as a potential solution to fix realization problems and finally get out of the stone age (or so it feels) right now we’re using PPC and ProSystem FX with a ton of manual checklists and processes, and it’s dragging us down on efficiency and we have pretty poor realization for our entire audit practice. Your insight really gives us a clearer picture of what we might be stepping into. I may follow up with some specific questions as we go further down this path. I may start looking at other alternatives… thank you again!

One last gripe, we can't link to the unadjusted accounts when we create workpapers in DAS and PCR. I'm not sure who thought this was a good idea, but instead of linking these, we have to hand jam the information into the workpapers. Ok, I'm all out of gripes.

Please take what I said with a large grain of salt. My firm had also been using PPC. Unlike PPC, the DAS workpapers sort of flow together... when they don't break. You know the pain of using PPC, where you make an update on one program and then you have to physically update several other programs to ensure they don't contradict one another. With DAS, everything should theoretically flow through. When you evaluate controls, the control report flows through to the risk assessment and risk response. When you make updates to the risk assessment, that information flows into your risk response.

Given that some time had passed would you still recommend OnpointPCR. I’m starting a solo firm and have been using CCH Engagement (ProFx) with PPC at my prior firm. The PCR is completely different product and seems foreign to me but I like that it’s cloud based and guided. I’m going to be doing monthly accounting work, comps and reviews. Is there also a function to export tax grouping schedule for tax prep? Thank you!

Hi. I believe DAS offers tax groupings, but I don’t think that feature is available for PCR. I believe we had and will continue to export adjusted TBs and then enter these into whatever tax platform we use.
For a solo practice or small shop firm, I’d recommend using PCR/DAS as it’s much more flexible than CCH and PPC. That said, there definitely is a learning curve.
